title: Wizards, Warlocks & Good vs. Evil
summary: Drop #6 introduces the 'Wizards and Warlocks' terminology that recurs throughout the Q canon. It frames the overall narrative as a battle of 'Good v. Evil' and asserts that 'more good people than bad' exist in government. It questions why certain information is 'pushed so hard.'
raw_text:
Why is the information being pushed so hard?
Why is this relevant?
There are more good people than bad.
The wizards and warlocks (inside term) protect us.
Good v. Evil.
Why is this relevant?

lines.text: Why is the information being pushed so hard?
lines.fact: No specific 'information' is identified in this drop, making verification impossible. The question implies that something is being actively suppressed or promoted, but no referent is given.
lines.theory: Q community members interpreted this as referring to various mainstream media narratives — at the time, coverage of Trump-Russia investigations was prominent. The framing encourages readers to view mainstream information with suspicion.
lines.context: The rhetorical technique of asking 'why is X being pushed?' without specifying X creates an open-ended suspicion of whatever the reader already distrusts. This is a cognitive priming technique studied in persuasion research.
lines.questions: What specific information is being referenced? Without an identifiable referent, the question cannot be evaluated.

lines.text: The wizards and warlocks (inside term) protect us.
lines.fact: 'Wizards and Warlocks' is a self-identified '(inside term)' — meaning Q acknowledges this is specialized jargon. No publicly available government, military, or intelligence document uses this terminology as an official designation. It cannot be verified as a real designation.
lines.theory: Q community members have speculated this refers to signals intelligence (SIGINT) specialists at the NSA, military cyber operators, or a specific classified unit. The parenthetical '(inside term)' was taken by followers as confirmation of insider access. No evidence has surfaced to confirm what group, if any, this refers to.
lines.context: The use of specialized-seeming jargon without verification is a technique that creates an impression of insider knowledge. Researchers studying Q note that unverifiable 'inside' language reinforces the impression of authenticity.
lines.questions: What organization or unit does this term refer to? Can it be verified through FOIA, congressional testimony, or declassified documents?
